label: Adam
definition: The default optimizer of deep learning, combining momentum with a per-parameter learning rate by tracking running averages of both the gradient and its square. Dividing by the second of these nearly cancels each gradient's scale, so every parameter takes a similarly sized step, which is why Adam works with almost no tuning.

body.text: The trouble with a single learning rate is that a network's parameters do not all want the same step size. Some sit in steep directions of the loss landscape where a large step overshoots; others sit in flat directions where the same step barely moves. Adam, short for adaptive moment estimation, gives every parameter its own effective rate, and it became the default optimizer of deep learning because it does so with almost no tuning. It tracks two running averages of each parameter's gradients: the first moment, the gradient's recent mean, and the second moment, the recent mean of its square.

The two moments do two jobs, and the second is where the real insight lives. The first moment is momentum: averaging recent gradients smooths the noisy trajectory and carries inertia along consistent directions. The second moment is the adaptive part: dividing each step by the square root of the recent squared gradient rescales it by how large that parameter's gradients have lately been. The striking consequence is that the size of a parameter's gradient nearly cancels out of its own update. A parameter with huge gradients and one with tiny gradients end up taking steps of roughly equal size, set by the learning rate alone. Adam is, in well-behaved regions, close to following just the sign of the smoothed gradient, which is exactly why it makes steady progress on every parameter at once and tolerates a learning rate that would be wrong for plain gradient descent.

Two details make it work. Both running averages start at zero, so for the first several steps they are biased toward zero; Adam divides by a bias-correction factor that undoes this and fades as the averages fill in. A small constant in the denominator keeps the division safe when a parameter's gradients have been near zero. The decay rates that set how much history each moment keeps are conventionally 0.9 for the first moment and 0.999 for the second, and those defaults rarely need changing, part of how Adam earned its reputation for simply working.

The version in standard use today is AdamW, which repairs how Adam handles weight decay: folding an L2 penalty into the gradient lets the same per-parameter rescaling distort the regularization, so AdamW instead shrinks the weights directly, outside the adaptive step, and the cleaner result generalizes better. The deeper caveat is the flip side of the convenience. Because Adam normalizes away gradient scale, it sometimes settles into sharper, worse-generalizing minima than a patiently tuned stochastic gradient descent with momentum, which is why some of the strongest vision results still come from SGD. Adam is the reliable default, not always the best answer: it trades a little final quality for a great deal of robustness, and knowing that trade is knowing when to reach past it.

image.alt: A descending, oscillating loss curve labeled momentum sits beside a balance scale weighing a pan marked gradient against a pan marked learning rate; the figure is titled Adam.
image.caption: A conceptual illustration of Adam, which combines momentum, a running average of past gradients that smooths the descent, with a per-parameter step size scaled to each weight's recent gradient magnitude. Balancing those two running estimates is what makes it robust enough to be the default optimizer for most deep networks.
